title: MSE vs RMSE vs MAE vs R²: Which Metric When?
concept: Choosing the right regression metric to report depends on the question you're asking: RMSE and MAE describe typical error size in real units, while R² gives a normalized score for comparing models across different datasets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does an R² close to 1 tell you about a regression model?

options.text: It explains most of the variance in the data compared to a naive average baseline

exercises.explain: Right — R² near 1 means the model explains most of the variance. Today: comparing all four regression metrics side by side.

cards.title: Four tools, one toolbox
cards.body: You've now met MSE, RMSE, MAE, and R². Reporting on a finished model isn't about picking a favorite — it's about knowing which tool answers which question.

cards.title: Four questions, four metrics
cards.body: "How big are the errors, in real units?" → RMSE or MAE. "How much does one huge miss matter?" → RMSE (squaring penalizes it more). "How does this model compare across different datasets?" → R².

cards.title: Vitals vs. a percentile score
cards.body: RMSE and MAE are like a patient's raw vital signs (heart rate: 72 bpm) — precise, in real units. R² is like a percentile score on a growth chart — it tells you how the model stacks up relative to a baseline, regardless of the raw units.

cards.title: Same model, four report lines
cards.body: A house-price model reports: MSE = 2.5B dollars², RMSE ≈ $50,000, MAE ≈ $38,000, R² = 0.85. Each line answers a different question — and MAE being noticeably lower than RMSE hints that a few large errors are dragging RMSE up.

cards.title: MAE isn't "worse," it's more robust
cards.body: It's tempting to think RMSE is always the "better" metric since it's stricter. But MAE's resistance to outliers makes it the more honest number when a few extreme misses shouldn't dominate the story.

cards.title: Pick your lens
cards.body: Two ways to summarize the same errors:

left.text:
Penalizes big misses heavily
Best when large errors are costly

right.text:
Treats every error proportionally
Best when outliers shouldn't dominate the story

exercises.prompt: A delivery-time model occasionally predicts wildly late deliveries, and those rare huge misses are the ones that matter most to the business. Which metric best highlights them?

options.text: RMSE, because squaring the errors penalizes those large misses much more heavily
MAE, because it treats all errors equally
R², because it ignores individual errors

exercises.explain: RMSE squares errors before averaging, so a few huge misses inflate it more than MAE — exactly what you want when big misses matter most.

exercises.prompt: True or false: R² is useful for comparing how well two models fit their own datasets, even if those datasets have very different scales.
exercises.explain: True! Because R² is a normalized 0-to-1 score, it stays meaningful for comparison even across datasets with different price ranges or units.

exercises.sentence: ___ is more robust to outliers than RMSE, because it doesn't square the errors before averaging.
exercises.explain: MAE averages absolute errors without squaring, so a few extreme errors don't dominate the score the way they do with RMSE.

exercises.prompt: You need to tell a non-technical stakeholder how good a house-price model is, in a way that's easy to compare to other projects they've seen. Which metric fits best?

options.text: R², since its 0-to-1 scale is easy to interpret as a percentage regardless of the price range
MSE, since squared dollars are intuitive to non-technical audiences
None of these — regression models can't be summarized in one number

exercises.explain: R²'s normalized scale makes it easy to compare across projects, even for someone unfamiliar with the underlying units.

exercises.prompt: A team trained two house-price models. Model A: RMSE = $45,000, MAE = $44,000, R² = 0.81. Model B: RMSE = $60,000, MAE = $35,000, R² = 0.83. Which conclusion best uses all three metrics together?

options.text: Model B has a few larger misses (a bigger gap between its RMSE and MAE than Model A's), but still explains slightly more overall variance (higher R²) — so the "better" choice depends on whether occasional big misses are costly
Model A is strictly better because its RMSE is lower, and the other two metrics can be ignored
Model B is strictly worse because its RMSE is higher, so no other numbers matter
The three metrics always agree, so any one of them alone tells the full story

exercises.explain: Exactly! No single metric tells the whole story — reading RMSE, MAE, and R² together reveals the full trade-off. Next: sometimes the clearest evaluation isn't a number at all — it's a picture.
